fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Data hazard mitigation: dynamically remap architectural register names to a larger pool of physical registers. False data dependencies — where two instructions write to the same architectural register but use independent values — get broken because the hardware can map each write to a different physical register.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Consider: add r1, r2, r3 // r2 := r1 + r3 add r4, r5, r2 // r2 := r4 + r5 (reuses r2 for an independent value) The second add only depends on r2 as a destination, not as a source. Without renaming, it would have to wait for the first instruction's writeback to avoid clobbering. With renaming, the second write goes to a fresh physical register, and the two instructions can proceed in parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The programming model exposes only the architecturally-named registers; the Register file hides a larger pool of rename registers underneath. This is one of the central tricks that makes out-of-order execution viable.

Multiple Execution unitss on the same chip running in parallel during the same pipeline stage. Decode becomes decode/dispatch: the front-end must decide which instructions can issue in parallel and route them to the right units. Going superscalar amplifies the cost of every pipeline Data hazard and Control hazard, which is why the rest of this chapter's machinery — Forwarding, Register renaming, Branch prediction — exists.
The functional circuitry that actually performs an instruction. A superscalar CPU contains several, dispatched to in parallel during the execute stage of the Pipelined execution. The common families:

html:ul.html:li: ALU — arithmetic logic unit, performs arithmetic (add, sub, mul, div) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations on integers and on special registers like the PSW. Typically split into integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).
Load-store unit — performs memory loads and stores. Carries a small address-calculation adder for address-mode resolution.
Branch execution unit — evaluates branch conditions (often by reading the PSW) and signals whether the branch is taken.
Vector / SIMD units — operate on wide registers (SSE, AVX, AltiVec) for data-parallel work.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Per the convention in Inside the Machine, "ALU" often refers to any execution unit for arithmetic or logical operations — including integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each unit's reads and writes flow through a Register file. Splitting the register file per-unit reduces port count and access time.

The internal organization of a CPU that implements a given instruction set architecture (ISA). Two chips can present identical ISAs to software while having radically different microarchitectures — e.g. Intel and AMD both implement x86, but their pipelines, branch predictors, and execution units differ.

The bridge between the two is the microcode engine: a mini-CPU inside the CPU. It holds small programs in a microcode ROM and runs them when a complex instruction needs to be translated into the simpler primitives the hardware actually executes. Conceptually, it's an emulator.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: CISC vs RISC-style ISAs need large microcode engines to implement their complex instructions. RISC designs minimize the microcode engine, pushing translation to the compiler instead.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The translated primitives are Micro-operations — a finer-grained, more uniform instruction stream that the actual hardware schedules. The translation step is the main reason an x86 chip's pipeline starts with such complex decode logic.

When one instruction's result is the next instruction's input, a naive Pipelined execution would have to stall: the second instruction can't execute until the first has written back. Data hazards are the most pervasive source of stalls in a CPU.
Two standard mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Forwarding — route the producer's output directly into the consumer's input, before the writeback stage commits it. The consumer sees the result one cycle earlier.

Register renaming — false dependencies, where two instructions write to the same architectural register but operate on independent values, can be broken by mapping the architectural register to two different physical registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Data hazard mitigation: the producing instruction's result is routed from the output of the execute stage directly to the input of the next instruction's execute stage, bypassing the register-file write and read. The dependent consumer can execute one cycle later instead of waiting for writeback.
Conceptually, the pipeline grows wiring rather than stages. The result is "forwarded" from where it appears to where it's needed.

A Data hazard mitigation: dynamically remap architectural register names to a larger pool of physical registers. False data dependencies — where two instructions write to the same architectural register but use independent values — get broken because the hardware can map each write to a different physical register.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Consider: add r1, r2, r3 // r2 := r1 + r3 add r4, r5, r2 // r2 := r4 + r5 (reuses r2 for an independent value) The second add only depends on r2 as a destination, not as a source. Without renaming, it would have to wait for the first instruction's writeback to avoid clobbering. With renaming, the second write goes to a fresh physical register, and the two instructions can proceed in parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The programming model exposes only the architecturally-named registers; the Register file hides a larger pool of rename registers underneath. This is one of the central tricks that makes out-of-order execution viable.

Two simultaneous instructions need the same hardware resource, and there's only one of it. The classic example is a single-port Register file facing two writes in one cycle, or two memory-accessing instructions facing a single load-store unit.
Mitigations cost area:

html:ul.html:li: Duplicate the resource (extra ALU, more register-file write ports).
Pipeline the resource (multi-cycle dividers that accept a new input each cycle).
Stall — accept the cost in throughput.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Register file area scales quadratically in port count, so duplicating ports for many ALUs gets expensive quickly.
Per-unit register files (one for integer, one for FP, one for vector) with fewer ports each. Discussed under Register file.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When the CPU reaches a Branch instruction in the Pipelined execution, it doesn't yet know what to fetch next. Until the branch's condition resolves and its target is computed — several stages later — fetch is stuck.
Mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Branch prediction — guess where control flow is going.
Speculative execution — actually run instructions down the predicted path, ready to throw the work away if the guess was wrong.
Instruction caching — keep recently-fetched instructions handy so the cost of restarting fetch on a mispredict is at least bounded by cache latency, not DRAM latency.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The cost of a mispredicted branch is roughly the pipeline depth — every speculatively-issued instruction has to be squashed and the pipeline refilled. Deeper pipelines amplify this cost, which is one reason Pentium 4 vs G4e: design philosophies (deep pipeline, narrow & deep) and Superscalar architecture-style designs (wider, shallower) make different tradeoffs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Execute instructions in whatever order their operands become available, then commit them back in program order. The programmer's model still sees sequential execution; the hardware reorders aggressively underneath to keep Execution units busy across Data hazards and memory latency.
Two structures make this work:

html:ul.html:li: Reservation stations — hold instructions waiting on operands, so a stalled instruction doesn't block independent ones behind it.
Reorder buffer — preserves program order so commits look sequential and mispredicted Speculative execution paths can be unwound.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Combined with Register renaming to break false dependencies, the back-end of a modern CPU is essentially a dynamic scheduler that uses the program order only as a hint about what to retire next.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: An out-of-order pipeline can issue a load early (because its address operands are ready), execute several unrelated ALU ops while waiting for the memory, then have all four instructions ready to commit in program order. The illusion of sequential execution is preserved; the parallelism is real.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The structure that lets an Out-of-order execution CPU look in-order to the programmer. The ROB holds every in-flight instruction in original program order, regardless of the order they actually executed. When the oldest entry has its result ready and all earlier entries have already committed, the ROB commits it — writing its result to architectural state and freeing the slot.
Each ROB entry typically tracks:

html:ul.html:li: Instruction identity and program order position.
Operand register names (and renamed sources).
Status — pending, executing, complete, speculated.
The instruction's result, held in a data field that doubles as a Register renaming register.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Speculatively-issued instructions also enter the ROB but are tagged. On a branch misprediction, all speculative entries past the mispredicted branch are squashed.

The Pentium Pro introduced this design with a 40-entry ROB. The ROB sits at both ends of the pipeline — dispatch enters it, retirement leaves it — which is what makes the rest of the back-end free to issue and execute out of order.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: ROB size, Reservation station count, and pipeline depth set each other's bounds. A shallow pipeline can afford a small ROB; a deep one (Pentium 4 had 126) needs a large one to keep the back-end fed.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Run instructions before knowing whether they should actually be run, on the assumption that you'll usually be right. Used after Branch prediction to keep the pipeline busy past a Branch instruction: instructions on the predicted path enter the pipeline, execute, and stash their results in non-architectural state. Only when the branch resolves are speculated results "committed" to architectural state — or, if the prediction was wrong, discarded.
Speculative state lives in the Reorder buffer (and the Register renaming rename registers). Entries are tagged as speculative; on misprediction the speculative entries are flushed.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Modern speculative execution drives a lot of CPU complexity — and a lot of CPU security work. Spectre-class side channels exploit the fact that speculative execution can leave traces in caches even after the speculative results are discarded.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When one instruction's result is the next instruction's input, a naive Pipelined execution would have to stall: the second instruction can't execute until the first has written back. Data hazards are the most pervasive source of stalls in a CPU.
Two standard mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Forwarding — route the producer's output directly into the consumer's input, before the writeback stage commits it. The consumer sees the result one cycle earlier.

Register renaming — false dependencies, where two instructions write to the same architectural register but operate on independent values, can be broken by mapping the architectural register to two different physical registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU's nearest tier of storage — the first NUMA level. Registers sit physically next to the ALUs and are addressed by name in the instruction encoding rather than by memory address. Their count is limited by the chip's surface area, which is why main memory exists at all.

The register file is the structure that holds the architecturally-visible registers. To support a superscalar machine, the file needs enough read and write ports to feed every ALU simultaneously: for an n-ALU chip with two operands per op, that's 2n read ports and n write ports.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Die area for a register file scales quadratically in the number of ports.
Split it. Different execution units (integer, floating-point, vector) get their own smaller register files. Fewer ports per file, faster access, less area.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Beyond the architecturally-named registers, modern chips keep many extra rename registers used by Register renaming to break false data dependencies.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Instructions distinguish "immediate types" from "register types" so the decoder knows whether a field encodes a register name or a literal value.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Machine-instance notes — concepts these chips introduced live in their own atomic notes; this tree records what was novel about each implementation.
Concepts in play:

html:ul.html:li: Cache hierarchy — Pentium adopted L1/L2 caching and split the L1 into separate instruction and data caches.
Branch prediction — branch history table (BHT) and branch target buffer (BTB) combined with static prediction as a fallback on BHT miss.
Speculative execution — execute past a predicted branch, write back only after the prediction is confirmed.

html:ol.html:li: Fetch / Prefetch: read from instruction cache.
Decode 1: decode to internal format; branch prediction.
Decode 2: convert complex instructions via Microarchitecture to Micro-operations.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Back-end: two integer ALUs (also doing address calculations) and one floating-point ALU. The FP register file was arranged as a stack, with most FP ops operating on the top — zero-cycle stack swaps existed but still occupied a decode slot.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Estimated 30% of Pentium transistors went to legacy support, mostly the microcode ROM.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Concepts introduced:

html:ul.html:li: Out-of-order execution — instructions execute in dataflow order, commit in program order.
Reorder buffer — 40-entry ROB tracks all in-flight instructions and assists Register renaming.
Reservation station — three-instruction dispatch, up to five-issue per cycle.
CISC vs RISC decoupling — x86 in front, Micro-operations internally.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Simple and complex decoders are split because many CISC instructions translate to one or two micro-ops — the simple decoders handle those in parallel with the slow complex decoder.
40% of Pentium Pro transistors went to backward-compatibility support.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Multiple Execution unitss on the same chip running in parallel during the same pipeline stage. Decode becomes decode/dispatch: the front-end must decide which instructions can issue in parallel and route them to the right units. Going superscalar amplifies the cost of every pipeline Data hazard and Control hazard, which is why the rest of this chapter's machinery — Forwarding, Register renaming, Branch prediction — exists.
The functional circuitry that actually performs an instruction. A superscalar CPU contains several, dispatched to in parallel during the execute stage of the Pipelined execution. The common families:

html:ul.html:li: ALU — arithmetic logic unit, performs arithmetic (add, sub, mul, div) and logical (AND, OR, NOT) operations on integers and on special registers like the PSW. Typically split into integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).
Load-store unit — performs memory loads and stores. Carries a small address-calculation adder for address-mode resolution.
Branch execution unit — evaluates branch conditions (often by reading the PSW) and signals whether the branch is taken.
Vector / SIMD units — operate on wide registers (SSE, AVX, AltiVec) for data-parallel work.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Per the convention in Inside the Machine, "ALU" often refers to any execution unit for arithmetic or logical operations — including integer logic units (ILU) and floating-point units (FPU).

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Each unit's reads and writes flow through a Register file. Splitting the register file per-unit reduces port count and access time.

The internal organization of a CPU that implements a given instruction set architecture (ISA). Two chips can present identical ISAs to software while having radically different microarchitectures — e.g. Intel and AMD both implement x86, but their pipelines, branch predictors, and execution units differ.

The bridge between the two is the microcode engine: a mini-CPU inside the CPU. It holds small programs in a microcode ROM and runs them when a complex instruction needs to be translated into the simpler primitives the hardware actually executes. Conceptually, it's an emulator.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: CISC vs RISC-style ISAs need large microcode engines to implement their complex instructions. RISC designs minimize the microcode engine, pushing translation to the compiler instead.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The translated primitives are Micro-operations — a finer-grained, more uniform instruction stream that the actual hardware schedules. The translation step is the main reason an x86 chip's pipeline starts with such complex decode logic.

When one instruction's result is the next instruction's input, a naive Pipelined execution would have to stall: the second instruction can't execute until the first has written back. Data hazards are the most pervasive source of stalls in a CPU.
Two standard mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Forwarding — route the producer's output directly into the consumer's input, before the writeback stage commits it. The consumer sees the result one cycle earlier.

Register renaming — false dependencies, where two instructions write to the same architectural register but operate on independent values, can be broken by mapping the architectural register to two different physical registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A Data hazard mitigation: the producing instruction's result is routed from the output of the execute stage directly to the input of the next instruction's execute stage, bypassing the register-file write and read. The dependent consumer can execute one cycle later instead of waiting for writeback.
Conceptually, the pipeline grows wiring rather than stages. The result is "forwarded" from where it appears to where it's needed.

A Data hazard mitigation: dynamically remap architectural register names to a larger pool of physical registers. False data dependencies — where two instructions write to the same architectural register but use independent values — get broken because the hardware can map each write to a different physical register.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Consider: add r1, r2, r3 // r2 := r1 + r3 add r4, r5, r2 // r2 := r4 + r5 (reuses r2 for an independent value) The second add only depends on r2 as a destination, not as a source. Without renaming, it would have to wait for the first instruction's writeback to avoid clobbering. With renaming, the second write goes to a fresh physical register, and the two instructions can proceed in parallel.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The programming model exposes only the architecturally-named registers; the Register file hides a larger pool of rename registers underneath. This is one of the central tricks that makes out-of-order execution viable.

Two simultaneous instructions need the same hardware resource, and there's only one of it. The classic example is a single-port Register file facing two writes in one cycle, or two memory-accessing instructions facing a single load-store unit.
Mitigations cost area:

html:ul.html:li: Duplicate the resource (extra ALU, more register-file write ports).
Pipeline the resource (multi-cycle dividers that accept a new input each cycle).
Stall — accept the cost in throughput.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Register file area scales quadratically in port count, so duplicating ports for many ALUs gets expensive quickly.
Per-unit register files (one for integer, one for FP, one for vector) with fewer ports each. Discussed under Register file.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When the CPU reaches a Branch instruction in the Pipelined execution, it doesn't yet know what to fetch next. Until the branch's condition resolves and its target is computed — several stages later — fetch is stuck.
Mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Branch prediction — guess where control flow is going.
Speculative execution — actually run instructions down the predicted path, ready to throw the work away if the guess was wrong.
Instruction caching — keep recently-fetched instructions handy so the cost of restarting fetch on a mispredict is at least bounded by cache latency, not DRAM latency.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: The cost of a mispredicted branch is roughly the pipeline depth — every speculatively-issued instruction has to be squashed and the pipeline refilled. Deeper pipelines amplify this cost, which is one reason Pentium 4 vs G4e: design philosophies (deep pipeline, narrow & deep) and Superscalar architecture-style designs (wider, shallower) make different tradeoffs.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: When one instruction's result is the next instruction's input, a naive Pipelined execution would have to stall: the second instruction can't execute until the first has written back. Data hazards are the most pervasive source of stalls in a CPU.
Two standard mitigations:

html:ul.html:li: Forwarding — route the producer's output directly into the consumer's input, before the writeback stage commits it. The consumer sees the result one cycle earlier.

Register renaming — false dependencies, where two instructions write to the same architectural register but operate on independent values, can be broken by mapping the architectural register to two different physical registers.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The CPU's nearest tier of storage — the first NUMA level. Registers sit physically next to the ALUs and are addressed by name in the instruction encoding rather than by memory address. Their count is limited by the chip's surface area, which is why main memory exists at all.

The register file is the structure that holds the architecturally-visible registers. To support a superscalar machine, the file needs enough read and write ports to feed every ALU simultaneously: for an n-ALU chip with two operands per op, that's 2n read ports and n write ports.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Die area for a register file scales quadratically in the number of ports.
Split it. Different execution units (integer, floating-point, vector) get their own smaller register files. Fewer ports per file, faster access, less area.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Beyond the architecturally-named registers, modern chips keep many extra rename registers used by Register renaming to break false data dependencies.

fr:tree.fr:mainmatter: Instructions distinguish "immediate types" from "register types" so the decoder knows whether a field encodes a register name or a literal value.
